planting.note: Plant container-grown divisions in spring in fertile, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; IMPORTANT: Install a 30-mil high-density polyethylene rhizome barrier at least 24–36 inches deep around the planting area before establishing any running bamboo to prevent invasive underground spread.

directions: Plant in fertile, moist, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade, contained within a rhizome barrier; Bisset bamboo is one of the most cold-hardy of all Phyllostachys species, reliably surviving temperatures to –10°F (–23°C) and making it an excellent choice for cold-climate gardens in zones 5–9. Its dense, upright culms reaching 15–20 feet create superb windbreaks, privacy screens, and living fences that remain evergreen through harsh winters. Like all running bamboos, it spreads aggressively via underground rhizomes and must be contained with a deep barrier or managed by annual rhizome pruning at the grove perimeter. The emerald-green culms hold their color well and are attractive for crafts and garden staking.

planting.note: Plant container-grown divisions in spring in fertile, moist, well-drained soil; install a 30-mil rhizome barrier 24–36 inches deep before planting. Yellow groove bamboo is among the easiest and most rewarding hardy bamboos for edible shoot production in zones 5–9.

directions: Plant in fertile, moist, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade, contained within a deep rhizome barrier; yellow groove bamboo is distinguished by its attractive yellow sulcus — a distinctive yellow groove running along one side of each otherwise green culm — making it one of the more ornamental of the hardy Phyllostachys species. The spring shoots are edible, milder than many bamboo species, and are considered among the better-tasting shoots of temperate bamboos; harvest them while still below knee height before sheaths open. Culms reach 15–25 feet at maturity and tolerate temperatures to –10°F (–23°C), rivaling Phyllostachys bissetii for cold hardiness. Containment with a rhizome barrier is mandatory, as this running bamboo spreads vigorously by underground rhizomes.

planting.note: Plant container-grown divisions in spring in humus-rich, moist, well-drained soil in partial shade; dragon head bamboo is a clumping species that does not spread invasively, making it one of the safest and most garden-friendly hardy bamboos for home landscapes.

directions: Plant in humus-rich, moist, well-drained soil in partial shade, sheltered from hot afternoon sun; dragon head bamboo is a Chinese clumping (pachymorph) Fargesia that forms a graceful, non-invasive arching clump 10–14 feet tall with abundant small, narrow leaves on slender culms — no rhizome barrier needed. Its cold hardiness to –10°F (–23°C) and preference for partial shade make it an outstanding choice for woodland gardens, shaded borders, and north-facing sites where few large ornamental plants thrive. Unlike many bamboos, Fargesia species prefer cooler summers and may show heat stress in humid southern climates; they are best suited to zones 5–9 with cool to moderate summers. The compact, tidy clumping habit makes it ideal for smaller gardens where running bamboos would be inappropriate.

planting.note: Plant divisions after all danger of frost has passed in spring in moist, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; palm grass is root-hardy in zones 9b and warmer and will resprout from the base after mild frost, but tops are killed by temperatures below 28°F.

directions: Plant in moist, organically rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade in zones 9–11, or grow as a container specimen brought indoors in colder climates; palm grass is a dramatic tropical ornamental grass from Southeast Asia and the Pacific, producing enormous pleated and corrugated leaves 2–4 feet long that closely resemble miniature palm fronds, creating a bold exotic statement in shaded tropical and subtropical gardens. It thrives in the conditions that challenge most ornamentals — deep shade, humid heat, and competition from tree roots — making it invaluable for tropical understory plantings. Caution: palm grass has naturalized and become invasive in parts of Florida, Hawaii, and other frost-free regions; check local regulations before planting in zones 10–11. In the tropics, the young shoots are used as a vegetable similar to bamboo shoots.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, humus-rich soil in shade to partial shade; kuma bamboo spreads by underground rhizomes and can colonize large areas over time — use as a vigorous groundcover or install a rhizome barrier to define boundaries.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained to consistently moist soil in shade to partial shade; kuma bamboo is a low-growing Japanese dwarf bamboo celebrated for its dramatic winter foliage effect — the leaf margins die back in cold weather to create a striking natural cream or parchment-colored border around each dark green blade, giving the plant an appearance of variegation through winter and early spring. This leaf withering is a natural adaptation, not a sign of damage, and the distinctive marginal color disappears when fresh green growth emerges in summer. Growing only 2–4 feet tall, it forms a dense, weed-suppressing groundcover ideal for shaded areas under trees, Japanese-style gardens, and naturalistic woodland settings in zones 6–9. Like other Sasa species, it spreads moderately by rhizomes and should be managed to prevent it from overrunning smaller companion plants.
